title: Global Fact summit: Countering authoritarian influence with quality journalism
content:
This year’s Global Fact event in Vilnius offered a broad showcase of the expertise of fact-checkers and investigative journalists. The major American digital platforms no longer participated in the event. We have compiled the main takeaways from the conference in this article.

Fact-checkers gathered during Midsummer week for the 13th international Global Fact summit in Vilnius, the capital of Lithuania. The political upheavals of recent years were reflected both in the composition of the event and in its themes. The presentations focused on the impact of artificial intelligence on information disorders and on democracies’ information defence against authoritarian influence.

There were somewhat fewer participants than before, as many newsrooms specialising in fact-checking have ceased their operations after the American digital giants Meta and Google’s owner Alphabet cut back on fact-checking across their social media services at the beginning of last year.

In the current political situation, responsibility for fact-checking and defending the integrity of the information environment is increasingly falling to European actors.
snippet: This year’s Global Fact event in Vilnius offered a broad showcase of the expertise of fact-checkers and investigative journalists. The major American digital platforms no longer participated in the
creator: Joonas Pörsti
sourceDescription: The Nordic Observatory for Digital Media and Information Disorder

title: NORDIS partner wins international award for impactful investigation
content:
NORDIS’ Danish fact-checking partner TjekDet has won the international “Global Fact-Checking Award 2026” together with the Danish newspaper Politiken, the Dutch outlet Bellingcat, and the Canadian broadcaster CBC at the award ceremony in Vilnius for their investigation exposing the person behind MrDeepFakes.

When the four media outlets published the investigation, MrDeepFakes was the world’s largest website for deepfake pornography, where well-known people – almost always women – were manipulated into pornographic videos with the help of artificial intelligence.

Among the victims were actors, TV presenters, influencers, and politicians.

The investigation led to the website shutting down in May last year. Before that, it was visited by several million users every single month.

“When we began this work, it was not only about finding the man behind the world’s largest deepfake pornography website. Above all, it was about the thousands of people whose faces, identities, and privacy were abused without their consent. That is why it means a great deal to us that this particular story is now being recognised internationally,” says TjekDet Editor-in-Chief Thomas Hedin.
snippet: NORDIS’ Danish fact-checking partner TjekDet has won the international “Global Fact-Checking Award 2026” together with the Danish newspaper Politiken, the Dutch outlet Bellingcat, and the Canadian
creator: Team NORDIS
sourceDescription: The Nordic Observatory for Digital Media and Information Disorder

title: When Denmark, Sweden, Norway or Finland hold elections or do anything that goes against the interests of Russia, hacktivists pester the Nordic countries – NORDIS has followed the hackers’ activities
content:
At a press conference last year the former Danish Minister of Defence, Troels Lund Poulsen, announced that Russia has been found responsible for “destructive and disruptive cyberattacks against Denmark”

The destructive attack took place in 2024 against a Danish waterworks facility, where hackers gained access to the control system and increased the water pressure. This caused several water pipes to burst and left residents without water.

The disruptive attacks were DDoS-attacks targeting Danish websites ahead of the 2025 municipal and regional elections, temporarily preventing access to the sites.

The attacks were carried out by the hacker groups Z-Pentest Alliance and NoName057(16), which, according to the Danish intelligence service, have links to the Russian state.
snippet: At a press conference last year the former Danish Minister of Defence, Troels Lund Poulsen, announced that Russia has been found responsible for “destructive and disruptive cyberattacks against
creator: Team NORDIS
sourceDescription: The Nordic Observatory for Digital Media and Information Disorder

title: Educating or performing? TikTok literacy activities in the Nordics
content:
TikTok’s popularity makes the platform both a significant site of online information disorders and a potentially powerful educator in media and information literacy. Yet, a monitoring study of the transparency reports shows that TikTok’s activities related to media and information literacy in the Nordics are highly limited and largely performative.

Authors: Minna Horowitz & Mervi Pantti. This article was first posted at Rajapinta.co.

TikTok has become a major global social media platform used for entertainment, communication, and increasingly for news. Its rapid growth has also made it a point of public concern, including impact on youth mental health, the viral spread of disinformation, and breaches of data and national security. These debates are prominent even in the Nordic countries, high-trust societies with advanced media and information literacy (MIL) policies and practices. MIL is generally understood as the ability to access, critically analyze, evaluate, create, and safely use media and information across all platforms.
snippet: TikTok’s popularity makes the platform both a significant site of online information disorders and a potentially powerful educator in media and information literacy. Yet, a monitoring study of the
creator: Team NORDIS
sourceDescription: The Nordic Observatory for Digital Media and Information Disorder

title: Almost everything is wrong: Google’s AI overview repeatedly provides incorrect information about AI-generated images
content:
NORDIS tested what kind of information Google’s AI overview provides for AI-generated images submitted through reverse image search. The results were concerning.

Reverse image search is one of the most important – if not the single most important – fact-checking tool. Fact-checkers, journalists, and ordinary citizens alike use it to investigate and verify images and videos circulating on social media. According to Google, people do over 100 billion visual searches a year with Google’s tools globally.

However, Google’s reverse image search now has a serious problem: according to tests conducted by NORDIS, the AI overview added to Google Search almost systematically provides incorrect information about images uploaded for reverse image search. Instead of helping with fact-checking, it obscures and complicates it.

AI overview also provides different information about the exact same image in nearly every test run, even if only a few seconds have passed between tests.
snippet: NORDIS tested what kind of information Google’s AI overview provides for AI-generated images submitted through reverse image search. The results were concerning.
creator: Team NORDIS
sourceDescription: The Nordic Observatory for Digital Media and Information Disorder

title: “We want him alive”: Danes behind bounty for capture of Iranian commentator
content:
“We are crowdfunding one million dollars as a bounty for the capture of Mohammad Marandi, an adviser to Iran’s leadership and a known IRGC propagandist. We want him alive.”

That is stated in a post from the X account “Terror Alarm,” which has more than 250,000 followers and circulated widely as a paid advertisement.

The post sparked international attention because it appears to encourage the kidnapping of an Iranian commentator — and because X allowed it to spread as sponsored content.

Mohammad Marandi is an Iranian professor and commentator who has appeared in Western media such as BBC and CNN and is known for supporting the Iranian regime.

Speculation has circulated online about whether Israel or the intelligence agency Mossad could be behind the account. The post was later removed following criticism, with “Terror Alarm” claiming Marandi himself had created it to gain attention.

However, research by NORDIS partner fact-checking outlet TjekDet shows that “Terror Alarm” is registered as a Danish association with a Copenhagen address and a Danish CVR number.
snippet: “We are crowdfunding one million dollars as a bounty for the capture of Mohammad Marandi, an adviser to Iran’s leadership and a known IRGC propagandist. We want him alive.”
creator: Team NORDIS
sourceDescription: The Nordic Observatory for Digital Media and Information Disorder

title: May 20, 2026: Climate Change Disinformation
content:
How does disinformation shape the public conversation about climate change?

This webinar explores how misleading narratives about climate change are created, spread and amplified online. The session is part of the European Immune 2 Infodemic project and is hosted by NORDIS fact-checking partner Faktabaari.

Participants will gain insights into common climate disinformation tactics and how journalists, educators and other professionals can identify and counter them.

The webinar is free, held in English, and open to all. It is particularly relevant for teachers, journalists, NGO professionals, policymakers and anyone interested in strengthening their resilience to misinformation.

Date & Time:
20 May, 15:00–16:00 CET

Format:
Online webinar

Organised by:
Immune 2 Infodemic (with Faktabaari)
snippet: How does disinformation shape the public conversation about climate change?
creator: Team NORDIS
sourceDescription: The Nordic Observatory for Digital Media and Information Disorder

title: May 13, 2026: Foreign Information Manipulation and Interference (FIMI)
content:
How do foreign actors manipulate information? And what does it mean for democratic societies?

This webinar focuses on Foreign Information Manipulation and Interference (FIMI) and how such campaigns are designed to influence public opinion across borders. The session is part of the European Immune 2 Infodemic project and is hosted by NORDIS fact-checking partner Faktabaari.

Participants will gain insights into how FIMI operations work in practice, how narratives are spread, and how journalists, educators and other professionals can respond.

The webinar is free, held in English, and open to all. It is particularly relevant for teachers, journalists, NGO professionals, policymakers and anyone interested in strengthening their resilience to misinformation.

Date & Time:
13 May, 15:00–16:00 CET

Format:
Online webinar

Organised by:
Immune 2 Infodemic (with Faktabaari)
snippet: How do foreign actors manipulate information? And what does it mean for democratic societies?
creator: Team NORDIS
sourceDescription: The Nordic Observatory for Digital Media and Information Disorder

title: April 29, 2026: AI-Enabled Disinformation
content:
How is artificial intelligence being used to create and spread disinformation?

This webinar explores the growing role of AI in today’s information environment and how it is reshaping the way misinformation is produced and distributed. The session is organised as part of the European Immune 2 Infodemic project and is hosted by NORDIS fact-checking partner Faktabaari.

The webinar features expert Markus Neuvonen, who will share insights into AI-assisted disinformation and how journalists, educators and other professionals can respond to it.

The session is free, held in English, and open to all. It is particularly relevant for teachers, journalists, NGO professionals, policymakers and anyone interested in strengthening their resilience to misinformation.

Date & Time:
29 April, 15:00–16:00 CET

Format:
Online webinar

Organised by:
Immune 2 Infodemic (with Faktabaari)
snippet: How is artificial intelligence being used to create and spread disinformation?
creator: Team NORDIS
sourceDescription: The Nordic Observatory for Digital Media and Information Disorder

title: No, Swedish minister does not want to introduce Islamic studies in schools
content:
False claims about the Swedish Liberals’ party leader Simona Mohamsson are spreading in English and Chinese.

A US Maga account with over three million followers has posted about Sweden’s Minister for Education and Integration Simona Mohamsson (L), and within a few days it has received nearly 40,000 interactions on Facebook, NORDIS-fact-checking parter Källkritikbyrån writes.

This is what the account wrote, translated into Swedish:

“Sweden’s current Minister for Education and Integration was born into a Muslim family, with her father being a former Hamas member in Palestine and her mother a Lebanese Shia Muslim.

She told the Swedes she is not Muslim, and the Swedes actually believed her! After taking office, she forcefully promoted Islamic studies courses, making them the highest-credit elective in all Swedish schools.

She demanded that all Swedish students must learn about Islam, saying Sweden needs Islam and only Islam can save Sweden.”

Facebook post, 19 April 2026

In the comment section, people write that Mohamsson does not look Swedish and that this is how immigrants take over the country.
snippet: False claims about the Swedish Liberals’ party leader Simona Mohamsson are spreading in English and Chinese.
creator: Team NORDIS
sourceDescription: The Nordic Observatory for Digital Media and Information Disorder
